questions.question: Hybridisation in methane (CH_4) ethene (C_2H_4) ethyne (C_2H_2) involves s and p orbitals. Choose the correct hybrid orbitals in the options given below for CH_4,_ C_2H_4 and C_2H_2 respectively:
questions.question_raw: Hybridisation in methane (CH4) ethene (C2H4) ethyne (C2H2) involves s and p orbitals. Choose the correct hybrid orbitals in the options given below for CH4, C2H4 and C2H2 respectively:
questions.explanation: In methane C is sp^3 hybridised because of 3 sigma bond formation. While in ethane both the C are sp^2 hybridised, and in ethyne both the C are sp hybridised.

questions.question: Change in hybridisation affects the carbon’s and the organic compound’s:
questions.question_raw: Change in hybridisation affects the carbon’s and the organic compound’s:
questions.explanation:
In hybridization as % of 's' character increases electronegativity also increases.
More the s character more will be the electronegativity of that atom.
Increasing order of electronegativity with %s character is sp (50%) > sp^2(33.33%) > sp^3(25%).

questions.question: Condense the following complete structural formula given below. Choose the appropriate answer given below
questions.question_raw: Condense the following complete structural formula given below. Choose the appropriate answer given below
questions.explanation: All the single bonds are broken and atoms are written together.

questions.question: 2-bromo butane can be represented in various ways. Choose the incorrect one from the following
questions.question_raw: 2-bromo butane can be represented in various ways. Choose the incorrect one from the following
questions.explanation: methyl group is represented at 3rd carbon no bromine is there on 2nd carbon

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct IUPAC name?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is the correct IUPAC name?
questions.explanation: While writing IUPAC name, alkyl groups are written in alphabetical order. thus lower locant 3 is assigned to ethyl. prefix di, tri or tetra are not included in alphabetical order

questions.question: Write the state of hybridisation of carbon in H_2C = O and mention the molecular geometry also.
questions.question_raw: Write the state of hybridisation of carbon in H2C = O and mention the molecular geometry also.
questions.explanation: Due to the presence of a double bond in the carbonyl carbon ( $>\mathrm C=\mathrm O$), carbon is sp^2 hybridised. This hybridization leads to a trigonal planar geometry which means a bond angle of 120° around the C.

questions.question: The IUPAC name for
questions.question_raw: The IUPAC name for
questions.explanation: The given compound contains chloro group at position 1, nitro at position 2 and methyl at position 3. So 1-Chloro-4-methyl-2-nitrobenzene is the IUPAC name of the given compound with groups named in alphabetical order.

questions.question: In $\pi$ (pi) bond formation, one of the below criteria is necessary. Choose the appropriate one.
questions.question_raw: In {tex}\pi{/tex} (pi) bond formation, one of the below criteria is necessary. Choose the appropriate one.

options.label: A
options.text: planar orientation of the two p orbitals on adjacent atoms.

options.label: B
options.text: pentagonal orientation of the two p orbitals on adjacent atoms.

options.label: C
options.text: trigonal orientation of the two p orbitals on adjacent atoms.

options.label: D
options.text: parallel orientation of the two p orbitals on adjacent atoms.

correct_option_texts: parallel orientation of the two p orbitals on adjacent atoms.
questions.explanation: Parallel orientation of p orbitals on adjacent atoms is necessary for side-ways overlapping and formation of $\pi$ bond.

questions.question: In general, in the molecules containing multiple bonds, ________ provide the most reactive centres. Choose the most appropriate one.
questions.question_raw: In general, in the molecules containing multiple bonds, ________ provide the most reactive centres. Choose the most appropriate one.
questions.explanation: $\pi$ bond formed by sideways overlapping of p orbitals/d orbitals on adjacent atoms provides the most reactive centers.

questions.question:
Which of the following compounds contain all the carbon atoms in the same hybridisation state?
 $H - C \equiv C - C \equiv C - H$
 $C{H_3} - C \equiv C - C{H_3}$
 CH_2 = C = CH_2
 $C{H_2} = CH - C \equiv CH$
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following compounds contain all the carbon atoms in the same hybridisation state?

  1. {tex}H - C \equiv C - C \equiv C - H{/tex}
  2. {tex}C{H_3} - C \equiv C - C{H_3}{/tex}
  3. CH2 = C = CH2
  4. {tex}C{H_2} = CH - C \equiv CH{/tex}
questions.explanation: In option A, all the 4 carbon atoms are sp-hybridized.

questions.question: Delocalisation of σ-electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p-orbital is called
questions.question_raw: Delocalisation of σ-electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p-orbital is called
questions.explanation: The interaction of the electrons in a sigma bond (usually C–H or C–C) with an adjacent empty (or partially filled) non-bonding p-orbital, antibonding $\sigma$ or $\pi$ orbital or filled $\pi$ orbital, to give an extended molecular orbital that increases the stability of the system is Hyperconjugation.

questions.question: Hyperconjugation in an organic compound is a
questions.question_raw: Hyperconjugation in an organic compound is a

options.label: B
options.text: delocalisation of σ electrons of C -C bond of an alkyl group

questions.explanation: Hyperconjugation is the stabilizing interaction that results from the interaction of the electrons in a C-H $\sigma$-bond with an adjacent empty p orbital or a $\pi$ orbital to give an extended molecular orbital that increases the stability of the system. It is a permanent effect.

questions.question: Covalent bonds are formed with other carbon atoms due to:
questions.question_raw: Covalent bonds are formed with other carbon atoms due to:
questions.explanation: Catenation is the linkage of atoms of the same element into longer chains. Catenation occurs most readily in carbon, which forms covalent bonds with other carbon atoms to form longer chains and structures. This is the reason for the presence of the vast number of organic compounds in nature.

questions.question: Which of the following organic compound Wohler synthesised from an inorganic compound, ammonium cyanate in the presence of heat?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following organic compound Wohler synthesised from an inorganic compound, ammonium cyanate in the presence of heat?
questions.explanation: The reaction can be given as starting with solutions of potassium cyanate and ammonium chloride which are mixed, heated and cooled again to get ammonium cyanate salt which on decomposition gives the urea.

questions.question:
Straight or branched chain compounds, for example, the below ones are called
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}\,\mathop {\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - CH - C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits^{\text{|}} }\limits^{{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \,\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - }}\mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^{{\text{||}}} }\limits^{\text{O}} {\text{ - H}}$
questions.question_raw:
Straight or branched chain compounds, for example, the below ones are called
{tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}\,\mathop {\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - CH - C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits^{\text{|}} }\limits^{{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \,\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - }}\mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^{{\text{||}}} }\limits^{\text{O}} {\text{ - H}}{/tex}
questions.explanation: The straight and branched chain compounds are known as aliphatic compounds (also called acyclic or open chain compounds).

questions.question: Correct IUPAC name for ${{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}\,{\text{ - }}\,\mathop {\mathop {{\text{CH}}}\limits_{\text{|}} }\limits_{{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}} \,{\text{ - }}\,\mathop {\mathop {{\text{CH}}}\limits_{\text{|}} }\limits_{{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}} \,{\text{ - }}\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}$ is ________.
questions.question_raw: Correct IUPAC name for {tex}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}\,{\text{ - }}\,\mathop {\mathop {{\text{CH}}}\limits_{\text{|}} }\limits_{{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}} \,{\text{ - }}\,\mathop {\mathop {{\text{CH}}}\limits_{\text{|}} }\limits_{{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}} \,{\text{ - }}\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{/tex} is ________.
questions.explanation: 3, 4 - Dimethylhexane will be correct IUPAC name as 6 membered carbon chain will be the parent chain with methyl groups at positions 3 and 4.

questions.question: Benzene and other related unsaturated ring compounds (benzenoid) such as given below are called:
questions.question_raw: Benzene and other related unsaturated ring compounds (benzenoid) such as given below are called:
questions.explanation: Aromatic compounds follow Huckels (4n+2)$\pi$ electrons rule. They can be benzenoid or non-benzenoid. They may also be heterocyclic.

questions.question: The following compound is:
questions.question_raw: The following compound is:
questions.explanation: Tropone or 2, 4, 6-cycloheptatriene-1-one is a non-benzenoid aromatic compound. The compound consists of a ring of seven carbon atoms with three conjugated alkene groups and a ketone group.

questions.question: Compounds containing carbon atoms joined together in the form of a ring as following are:
questions.question_raw: Compounds containing carbon atoms joined together in the form of a ring as following are:
questions.explanation: An alicyclic compound is an organic compound that is both aliphatic and cyclic. They contain one or more all-carbon rings which may be either saturated or unsaturated, but do not have aromatic character.

questions.question: The molecular formula C_3H_8O represents two alcohols: propan-1-ol and propan-2-ol. This property is called as:
questions.question_raw: The molecular formula C3H8O represents two alcohols: propan-1-ol and propan-2-ol. This property is called as:
questions.explanation: propan-1-ol and propan-2-ol are positional isomers as they differ only in the position of functional group -OH. Positional isomers are isomers that have the same carbon skeleton and the same functional groups but differ from each other in the location of the functional groups on or in the carbon chain.

questions.question: Propanal and propanone are
questions.question_raw: Propanal and propanone are
questions.explanation: Two or more compounds having the same molecular formula but different functional groups are called functional isomers and this phenomenon is termed as functional group isomerism. For example, the molecular formula C_3H_6O represents both a ketone and aldehyde:$CH_3COCH_3\; and\;CH_3CH_2CHO$

questions.question: What is the type of hybridisation of each carbon in the compound (CH_3)_2CO?
questions.question_raw: What is the type of hybridisation of each carbon in the compound (CH3)2CO?
questions.explanation: The hybridisation of the C in CH_3 (all are single bonds) is sp^3 and that of C of $>\mathrm C=\mathrm O$ (double bond) bond is sp^2.

questions.question: Alkyl radical (Methyl, Ethyl, Isopropyl, Tert-butyl free radical) stability in the increasing order is:
questions.question_raw: Alkyl radical (Methyl, Ethyl, Isopropyl, Tert-butyl free radical) stability in the increasing order is:
questions.explanation: Tertiary radical is more stable than secondary, primary and methyl. Alkyl groups directly attached to the carbon with unpaired electron stabilise the carbocations due to inductive and hyperconjugation effects.

questions.question: The nitrogen containing organic compound, when heated with copper oxide in an atmosphere of carbon dioxide, yields:
questions.question_raw: The nitrogen containing organic compound, when heated with copper oxide in an atmosphere of carbon dioxide, yields:

options.label: C
options.text: free nitrogen in addition to carbondioxide and water.

correct_option_texts: free nitrogen in addition to carbondioxide and water.
questions.explanation:
In the Dumas method for estimation of nitrogen in an organic compound, the nitrogen-containing organic compound, when heated with copper oxide in an atmosphere of carbon dioxide, yields free nitrogen in addition to carbon dioxide and water.
$C_xH_yN_z\;+\;\left(x+\frac y2\right)CuO\rightarrow xCO_2\;+\frac y2\;H_2O\;+\frac z2N_2\;+\left(2x+\frac y2\right)Cu$

questions.question: A known mass of an organic compound is heated with fuming nitric acid in the presence of silver nitrate. This is
questions.question_raw: A known mass of an organic compound is heated with fuming nitric acid in the presence of silver nitrate. This is
questions.explanation: The Carius method in analytical chemistry is a method for the quantitative determination of halogens in organic substances. It involves heating a known mass of an organic compound with fuming nitric acid in the presence of silver nitrate contained in a hard glass tube known as carius tube, in a furnace. The halogen present forms the corresponding silver halide (AgX). It is filtered, washed, dried, and weighed.

questions.question: Carius tube is used in the estimations of which one of the following?
questions.question_raw: Carius tube is used in the estimations of which one of the following?
questions.explanation: It is used in the estimation of Halogen and Sulphur. For estimation of sulphur a known mass of an organic compound is heated in a hard glass tube called a Carius tube with sodium peroxide or fuming nitric acid. For estimation of halogen, a known mass of an organic compound is heated with fuming nitric acid in the presence of silver nitrate contained in a Carius tube, in a furnace.

questions.question: In the naming of organic compounds containing polyfunctional compounds, one of the functional groups is chosen as the principal functional group and the compound is then named on that basis. The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups is:
questions.question_raw: In the naming of organic compounds containing polyfunctional compounds, one of the functional groups is chosen as the principal functional group and the compound is then named on that basis. The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups is:

options.label: A
options.text: - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - NH_2, > C = C <, - C $\equiv$ C -, -CONH_2

options.label: B
options.text: - C = O, - OH, - NH_2, > C = C <, - C $\equiv$ C -, -CONH_2, - CN > - HC = O

options.label: D
options.text: - HC = O, > C = O, -OH, -NH_2, > C = C <, - C $\equiv $C -, -CONH_2, - CN

questions.explanation:
The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups is:
-CONH_2, -CN, -HC = O, > C = O, -OH, -NH_2, > C = C <, -C$\equiv$C-
Carboxlic acid > sulphonic acid > acid anhydride > ester > acyl halide > acyl amide > nitrile > aldehyde > ketones > alcohol > amine > alkene > alkyne.

questions.question: If an –NH_2 functional group is present in an organic compound it is called amine. If a -CONH_2 functional group is present in an organic compound then it is called:
questions.question_raw: If an –NH2 functional group is present in an organic compound it is called amine. If a -CONH2 functional group is present in an organic compound then it is called:
questions.explanation: -CONH_2 is amide functional group. Amides are formed by conversion of a carboxylic acid into ammonium salt and then heating it.

questions.question: A covalent bond when cleaved heterolytically yields:
questions.question_raw: A covalent bond when cleaved heterolytically yields:
questions.explanation: Heterolytic cleavage produces carbonium ion and carbanion. In heterolytic cleavage, the bond breaks in such a fashion that the shared pair of electrons remains with one of the fragments. After heterolysis, one atom has a sextet electronic structure and a positive charge and the other, a valence octet with at least one lone pair and a negative charge. A species having a carbon atom possessing electrons and a positive charge is called a carbocation (earlier called carbonium ion). There is also a carbon species carrying a negative charge on a carbon atom which gets the unshared pair of electrons which is called carbanion.

questions.question: Electronegativity of carbon atoms depends upon their state of hybridisation. In which of the following compounds, the carbon marked with asterisk is most electronegative?
questions.question_raw: Electronegativity of carbon atoms depends upon their state of hybridisation. In which of the following compounds, the carbon marked with asterisk is most electronegative?
questions.explanation: Greater is the s character of the hybridised orbital, greater is the electronegativity of the atom. In a triply bonded C, there is sp hybridisation. In sp hybridization, the atom has maximum s character (50%). So in this case the carbon with a triple bond has maximum electronegativity.

tests.testPaperName: Organic Chemistry Some Basic Principles CBSE Online Test 03
tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Chemistry > Organic Chemistry Some Basic Principles and Techniques

questions.question: In which of the following, functional group isomerism is not possible?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following, functional group isomerism is not possible?
questions.explanation: Two or more compounds having the same molecular formula but different functional groups are called functional isomers and this phenomenon is termed as functional group isomerism. Alkyl halides do not show functional isomerism.

questions.question: The fragrance of flowers is due to the presence of some steam volatile organic compounds called essential oils. These are generally insoluble in water at room temperature but are miscible with water vapour in vapour phase. A suitable method for the extraction of these oils from the flowers is:
questions.question_raw: The fragrance of flowers is due to the presence of some steam volatile organic compounds called essential oils. These are generally insoluble in water at room temperature but are miscible with water vapour in vapour phase. A suitable method for the extraction of these oils from the flowers is:
questions.explanation: Steam distillation is a technique applied to separate substances which are steam volatile and are immiscible with water. Essential oils are insoluble in water and soluble in steam and have high vapour pressure. Therefore, they can be separated by steam distillation.

questions.question: During hearing of a court case, the judge suspected that some changes in the documents had been carried out. He asked the forensic department to check the ink used at two different places. According to you which technique can give the best results?
questions.question_raw: During hearing of a court case, the judge suspected that some changes in the documents had been carried out. He asked the forensic department to check the ink used at two different places. According to you which technique can give the best results?
questions.explanation: Thin layer chromatography (TLC) is an adsorption chromatography, which involves separation of substances of a mixture over a thin layer of an adsorbent coated on glass plate. It is used to separate the pigments of the ink and will give the best results.

questions.question: The principle involved in paper chromatography is
questions.question_raw: The principle involved in paper chromatography is
questions.explanation: Paper chromatography is a type of partition chromatography. In paper chromatography, a special quality paper known as chromatography paper is used. Chromatography paper contains water trapped in it, which acts as the stationary phase. Separation of components of a mixture depends upon their partitioning between water held in stationary phase (adsorbant paper) and the liquid present in the mobile phase.

questions.question: In which of the following compounds the carbon marked with asterisk is expected to have greatest partial positive charge?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following compounds the carbon marked with asterisk is expected to have greatest partial positive charge?
questions.explanation: Cl is most electronegative amongst Cl, Br and I and has more -I (inductive electron withdrawing) effect. So it causes the asterisk marked to have the maximum partial positive charge.

questions.question: C_4H_10O represents methoxypropane CH_3OC_3H_7 and ethoxyethane (C_2H_5OC_2H_5):
questions.question_raw: C4H10O represents methoxypropane CH3OC3H7 and ethoxyethane (C2H5OC2H5):
questions.explanation: Metamerism arises due to different alkyl chains on either side of the functional group in the molecule. For example, C_4H_10O represents methoxypropane (CH_3OC_3H_7) and ethoxyethane (C_2H_5OC_2H_5).

questions.question: The organic compound given below is one of the four options given below. Choose the most appropriate one.
questions.question_raw: The organic compound given below is one of the four options given below. Choose the most appropriate one.
questions.explanation: The aliphatic heterocyclic compounds are the cyclic analogues of amines, ethers, thioethers and their properties are influenced by the ring strain.

questions.question: The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups in the naming of an organic compound is:
questions.question_raw: The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups in the naming of an organic compound is:

options.label: A
options.text: - CONH_2, - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - NH_2, - COOR (R = alkyl group), - COCl

options.label: B
options.text: - COOR (R = alkyl group right), - COCl, - CONH_2 , - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - H_2

options.label: C
options.text: - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - NH_2, - COOR (R = alkyl group), - COCl, - CONH_2

options.label: D
options.text: - COCl, - CONH_2, - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - NH_2, - COOR (R = alkyl group)

correct_option_texts: - COOR (R = alkyl group right), - COCl, - CONH_2 , - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - H_2
questions.explanation:
COOR (R=alkyl group) > -COCl,> -CONH_2 > -CN > -HC=O> >C=O> -OH, -NH_2 is the decreasing order of priority
ester > acyl halide > acyl amide > nitrile > aldehyde > ketones > alcohol > amine

questions.question:
Select the nucleophile(s) among the following molecules/ions
HS^-, BF_3, C_2H_5O^-, (CH_3)_3N:, Cl^+, CH_3C^+ = O, H_2N^-:, NO_2^​​​​+
questions.question_raw:
Select the nucleophile(s) among the following molecules/ions
HS-, BF3, C2H5O-, (CH3)3N:, Cl+, CH3C+ = O, H2N-:, NO2​​​​+
questions.explanation: Nucleophiles are nucleus loving species. These can be negatively charged or netural having lone pair of electrons. So among the given options nucleophiles are HS^-, H_2N^-:, (CH_​​​​​3)_3N:, C_2H_5O^-

questions.question:
Select the electrophile(s) among the following molecules/ions
${\text{H}}{{\text{S}}^{\text{ - }}}{\text{,}}\,{\text{B}}{{\text{F}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{,}}\,{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}{{\text{O}}^{\text{ - }}}{\text{,}}\,{{\text{(C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{)}}_{\text{3}}}\,{\text{N:,}}\,{\text{C}}\mathop {\text{l}}\limits^{\text{ + }} {\text{,}}\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\mathop {\text{ - }}\limits_{\text{3}} }}\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^{\text{ + }} \,{\text{ = }}\,{\text{O,}}\,{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{N}}\mathop {\text{:}}\limits^{\text{ - }} {\text{,}}\,\,\mathop {\text{N}}\limits^{\text{ + }} \,{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}$
questions.question_raw:
Select the electrophile(s) among the following molecules/ions
{tex}{\text{H}}{{\text{S}}^{\text{ - }}}{\text{,}}\,{\text{B}}{{\text{F}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{,}}\,{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}{{\text{O}}^{\text{ - }}}{\text{,}}\,{{\text{(C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{)}}_{\text{3}}}\,{\text{N:,}}\,{\text{C}}\mathop {\text{l}}\limits^{\text{ + }} {\text{,}}\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\mathop {\text{ - }}\limits_{\text{3}} }}\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^{\text{ + }} \,{\text{ = }}\,{\text{O,}}\,{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{N}}\mathop {\text{:}}\limits^{\text{ - }} {\text{,}}\,\,\mathop {\text{N}}\limits^{\text{ + }} \,{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{/tex}
questions.explanation: Electrophiles are electron loving species. These can be electron deficient neutral molecules or positively charged ions. Thus BF_3, Cl^+, NO_2^+, CH_3-C^+=O are electrophiles.

questions.question: Inorganic compounds, when homolytic cleavage takes place, one of the electrons of the shared pair in a covalent bond goes with each of the bonded atoms. This results in the formation of:
questions.question_raw: Inorganic compounds, when homolytic cleavage takes place, one of the electrons of the shared pair in a covalent bond goes with each of the bonded atoms. This results in the formation of:
questions.explanation: In homolytic cleavage or homolysis, the two electrons in a cleaved covalent bond are divided equally between the products. Thus, in homolytic cleavage, the movement of a single electron takes place instead of an electron pair. This process is also known as homolytic fission. Such cleavage results in the formation of neutral species (atom or group) which contains an unpaired electron. These species are called free radicals.

questions.question: The following compounds are called:
questions.question_raw: The following compounds are called:
questions.explanation: Aromatic compounds may also have heteroatom in the ring. Such compounds are called heterocyclic aromatic compounds. Thus the given compounds are aromatic heterocyclic compounds namely furan, thiophene and pyridine respectively.

questions.question: In which bond of CH_3CH_2CH_2Br the inductive effect is expected to be the least?
questions.question_raw: In which bond of CH3CH2CH2Br the inductive effect is expected to be the least?
questions.explanation: As the distance of the functional group increases its inductive effect decreases. Carbon-3 is farthest from Br. So in the bond between carbon-2 and carbon-3 the inductive effect is the least.

questions.question: To separate mixtures into their components, and also to test the purity of compounds, the popularly used technique is:
questions.question_raw: To separate mixtures into their components, and also to test the purity of compounds, the popularly used technique is:
questions.explanation: Chromatography is used for the purpose given. In this technique the mixture of substances is applied onto a stationary phase, which may be a solid or a liquid. A pure solvent, a mixture of solvents, or a gas is allowed to move slowly over the stationary phase. The components of the mixture get gradually separated from one another.

questions.question: The elements present in an organic compound are converted from covalent form into the ionic form by fusing the compound with sodium metal. This test is called:
questions.question_raw: The elements present in an organic compound are converted from covalent form into the ionic form by fusing the compound with sodium metal. This test is called:
questions.explanation: Nitrogen, sulphur, halogens, and phosphorus present in an organic compound are detected by “Lassaigne’s test”. The elements present in the compound are converted from the covalent form into the ionic form by fusing the compound with sodium metal. Cyanide, sulphide and halide of sodium so formed on sodium fusion are extracted from the fused mass by boiling it with distilled water. This extract is known as sodium fusion extract.

tests.testPaperName: Organic Chemistry Some Basic Principles CBSE Online Test 04
tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Chemistry > Organic Chemistry Some Basic Principles and Techniques

questions.question: The principle of partition chromatography is:
questions.question_raw: The principle of partition chromatography is:

options.label: A
options.text: separation of a mixture over a column of the adsorbent.

options.label: B
options.text: separation of ions and polar molecules based on their affinity to ion exchanger.

options.label: C
options.text: separation of substances of a mixture over a thin layer of an adsorbent.

options.label: D
options.text: continuous differential partitioning of components of a mixture.

correct_option_texts: continuous differential partitioning of components of a mixture.
questions.explanation: Partition chromatography is based on the continuous differential partitioning of components of a mixture between stationary and mobile phases. Paper chromatography is a type of partition chromatography. In paper chromatography, a special quality paper known as chromatography paper is used. Chromatography paper contains water trapped in it, which acts as the stationary phase.

questions.question: Aniline is separated from aniline-water mixture using following technique:
questions.question_raw: Aniline is separated from aniline-water mixture using following technique:
questions.explanation: Steam distillation is a special type of distillation (a separation process) for temperature-sensitive materials like natural aromatic compounds. It was a popular laboratory method for purification of organic compounds.

questions.question: Sodium cyanide, sulphide, and halide, (-CN, -S, and -H, coming from the organic compound) formed on fusion with sodium are extracted from the fused mass by boiling it with distilled water. This is called:
questions.question_raw: Sodium cyanide, sulphide, and halide, (-CN, -S, and -H, coming from the organic compound) formed on fusion with sodium are extracted from the fused mass by boiling it with distilled water. This is called:
questions.explanation: Nitrogen, sulphur, halogens, and phosphorus present in an organic compound are detected by “Lassaigne’s test”. The elements present in the compound are converted from the covalent form into the ionic form by fusing the compound with sodium metal. Cyanide, sulphide, and halide of sodium so formed on sodium fusion are extracted from the fused mass by boiling it with distilled water. This extract is known as sodium fusion extract.

questions.question: Geometrical isomers and Optical isomers are:
questions.question_raw: Geometrical isomers and Optical isomers are:
questions.explanation: They are stereoisomers as they differ in the orientation of atoms in space.

questions.question: When an organic compound is present in an aqueous medium, it is separated by:
questions.question_raw: When an organic compound is present in an aqueous medium, it is separated by:
questions.explanation: Differential extraction or solvent extraction is an experimental technique where an organic compound can be isolated from its aqueous solution by shaking it with an organic solvent. The organic compound will dissolve in the organic solvent, and with the use of a separating funnel, the organic solvent and water mixture is separated. The organic compound is obtained by evaporating the organic solvent.

questions.question:
Acid catalysed electrophilic addition reaction to carbon-carbon double bond proceeds in two steps. The first step involves the addition of an H^+ to double bond. Name the type of intermediate formed in the first step of the following addition reaction:
${{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}-{\text{HC }} = {\text{ C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}} + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}^ + } \to ?$
questions.question_raw:
Acid catalysed electrophilic addition reaction to carbon-carbon double bond proceeds in two steps. The first step involves the addition of an H+ to double bond. Name the type of intermediate formed in the first step of the following addition reaction:
{tex}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}-{\text{HC }} = {\text{ C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}} + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}^ + } \to ?{/tex}
questions.explanation: In this reaction, formation of secondary carbocation takes place by the addition of H^+ to terminal carbon of carbon-carbon double bond among the two possible carbon atoms. The more stable secondary carbocation formation will take place instead of primary carbocation.

questions.question: Polarisation of $\sigma$-bond caused by the polarisation of adjacent $\sigma$-bond is termed as:
questions.question_raw: Polarisation of {tex}\sigma{/tex}-bond caused by the polarisation of adjacent {tex}\sigma{/tex}-bond is termed as:
questions.explanation: Polarisation of $\sigma$-bond caused by the polarisation of the adjacent $\sigma$-bond is referred to as the inductive effect. This effect is passed on to the subsequent bonds also but the effect decreases rapidly as the number of intervening bonds increases and becomes vanishingly small after three bonds. The inductive effect is related to the ability of substituent(s) to either withdraw or donate electron density to the attached carbon atom.

questions.question: Covalent bond can undergo fission in two different ways. The correct representation involving a heterolytic fission of ${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}-{\text{Br}}$ is:
questions.question_raw: Covalent bond can undergo fission in two different ways. The correct representation involving a heterolytic fission of {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}-{\text{Br}}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation: Arrows are shown in the direction of movement of electrons. Bromine (Br) being more electronegative attracts the shared paired of electrons and results in formation of Br^- ion and ^+CH_3​.

questions.question:
The addition of HCl to alkene proceeds in two steps. The first step is the attack of H+ ion to the portion which can be shown as:
All of these are possible
questions.question_raw:
The addition of HCl to alkene proceeds in two steps. The first step is the attack of H+ ion to  the portion which can be shown as:

  4. All of these are possible
questions.explanation: Since arrows are shown in the direction of movement of electrons, so the correct depiction is B.

questions.question: For testing halogens in an organic compound with ${\text{AgN}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}$solution, sodium extract (Lassaigne’s test) is acidified with dilute ${\text{HN}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}.$What will happen if a student acidifies the extract with dilute ${{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}$in place of dilute HNO_3?
questions.question_raw: For testing halogens in an organic compound with {tex}{\text{AgN}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}{/tex}solution, sodium extract (Lassaigne’s test) is acidified with dilute {tex}{\text{HN}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}.{/tex}What will happen if a student acidifies the extract with dilute {tex}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}{/tex}in place of dilute HNO3?

options.label: A
options.text: White ppt. of ${\text{A}}{{\text{g}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}$ will be formed

questions.explanation: The sodium fusion extract is acidified with nitric acid and then treated with silver nitrate. A white precipitate, soluble in ammonium hydroxide shows the presence of chlorine, a yellowish precipitate, sparingly soluble in ammonium hydroxide shows the presence of bromine and a yellow precipitate, insoluble in ammonium hydroxide shows the presence of iodine. If instead of nitric acid, sulphuric acid is used then white precipitate of ${\text{A}}{{\text{g}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}$ is formed.

questions.question: Delocalisation of σ electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital is called:
questions.question_raw: Delocalisation of σ electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital is called:
questions.explanation: Delocalisation of σ electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital is known as Hyperconjugation. It is a general stabilising interaction. The $\sigma$ electrons of C- H bond of the alkyl group enter into partial conjugation with the attached unsaturated system or with the unshared p orbital. It is a permanent effect.

questions.question: In which of the following representations given below spatial arrangement of group/ atom different from that given in structure A?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following representations given below spatial arrangement of group/ atom different from that given in structure A?
questions.explanation: By rotation of A around vertical axis B and around horizontal axis D can be obtained. But A and C be obtained only by breaking of bonds.

questions.question: Why Kjeldahl method cannot be used for the estimation of nitrogen present in DNA and RNA? Choose the most appropriate option.
questions.question_raw: Why Kjeldahl method cannot be used for the estimation of nitrogen present in DNA and RNA? Choose the most appropriate option.

options.label: A
options.text: DNA and RNA have phosphorous which interferes in the Kjeldahl method

options.label: B
options.text: Nitrogen present in rings, azo groups and nitro groups of DNA and RNA cannot be removed

options.label: C
options.text: DNA and RNA have deoxyribose and ribose which interferes in the Kjeldahl method

options.label: D
options.text: The bases present in the DNA and RNA structures do not allow them to participate in the Kjeldahl method

correct_option_texts: Nitrogen present in rings, azo groups and nitro groups of DNA and RNA cannot be removed
questions.explanation: Kjeldahl method is not applicable to compounds containing nitrogen in nitro and azo groups and nitrogen present in the ring (e.g. pyridine) as nitrogen of these compounds does not change to ammonium sulphate under these conditions. So it is not used to estimate Nitrogen in DNA and RNA.

questions.question: Which one of the following compounds is used in the estimation of oxygen?
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following compounds is used in the estimation of oxygen?
questions.explanation: I_2O_5 is used. A definite mass of an organic compound is decomposed by heating in a stream of nitrogen gas. The mixture of gaseous products containing oxygen is passed over red-hot coke when all the oxygen is converted to carbon monoxide. This mixture is passed through warm iodine pentoxide when carbon monoxide is oxidised to carbon dioxide producing iodine.

questions.question:
Electrophiles are electron seeking species. Which of the following groups contain only electrophile
 ${\text{B}}{{\text{F}}_{\text{3}}},{\text{ N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}},{\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}$
 $AlCl_3, SO_3,NO_2^ + $
 ${\text{NO}}_2^ + ,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}^ + ,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}} - \mathop C\limits^ + = {\text{O}}$
 ${{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{H}}_5^ + , CH_3^+, NH_2^-$
questions.question_raw:
Electrophiles are electron seeking species. Which of the following groups contain only electrophile

  1. {tex}{\text{B}}{{\text{F}}_{\text{3}}},{\text{ N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}},{\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}AlCl_3, SO_3,NO_2^ + {/tex}
  3. {tex}{\text{NO}}_2^ + ,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}^ + ,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}} - \mathop C\limits^ + = {\text{O}}{/tex}
  4. {tex}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{H}}_5^ + , CH_3^+, NH_2^-{/tex}
questions.explanation: Electrophile are electron loving species which can be positively charged ions or neutral molecules which are electron deficient. (ii) and (iii) contains all the electrophiles.

questions.question:
In the four compounds below, which of the following pairs are position isomers?
 $C{H_3} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - H$
 $C{H_3} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}$
 $C{H_3} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_{||} }\limits_O - C{H_2} - C{H_3}$
 $C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{C{H_3}} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_{||} }\limits_O - H$
questions.question_raw:
In the four compounds below, which of the following pairs are position isomers?

  1. {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - H{/tex}
  2. {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}{/tex}
  3. {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_{||} }\limits_O - C{H_2} - C{H_3}{/tex}
  4. {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{C{H_3}} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_{||} }\limits_O - H{/tex}
questions.explanation: B and C are position isomers as they have the same number of C atoms and same functional group -C=O but the position of carbonyl group is C-2 in B and C-3 in C.

questions.question: In Carius method, the organic compound is heated with
questions.question_raw: In Carius method, the organic compound is heated with
questions.explanation: In carius method of estimation of halogens, aknown mass of an organic compound is heated with fuming nitric acid in the presence of silver nitrate contained in a hard glass tube known as Carius tube, in a furnace.

questions.question: Write the state of hybridisation of carbon in the compound, $HC \equiv N$ and shape of the molecule.
questions.question_raw: Write the state of hybridisation of carbon in the compound, {tex}HC \equiv N{/tex} and shape of the molecule.
questions.explanation: As the C is bonded to N by a triple bond so it is sp hybridized andand the molecule is linear in shape.

questions.question: In sulphur estimation, 0.157 g of an organic compound gave 0.4813 g of barium sulphate. What is the percentage of sulphur in the compound?
questions.question_raw: In sulphur estimation, 0.157 g of an organic compound gave 0.4813 g of barium sulphate. What is the percentage of sulphur in the compound?
questions.explanation:
Molecular mass of BaSO_4 = 137 + 32 + 64 g = 233 g
233 g BaSO_4 contains 32 g S
0.4813 g BaSO_4 contains $\frac{{(32 \times 0.4813g)}}{{233\;S}}$
So % of S = $ \frac{{(32 \times 0.4813 \times 100)}}{{(233 \times 0.157)}}$
S = 42.10%

questions.question: During estimation of nitrogen present in an organic compound by Kjeldahl’s method, the ammonia evolved from 0.5 g of the compound, neutralized 10 mL of 1 M H_2SO_4. Find out the percentage of nitrogen in the compound.
questions.question_raw: During estimation of nitrogen present in an organic compound by Kjeldahl’s method, the ammonia evolved from 0.5 g of the compound, neutralized 10 mL of 1 M H2SO4. Find out the percentage of nitrogen in the compound.
questions.explanation:
1 M of 10 mL H_2SO_4= 1 M of 20 mL NH_3
1000 mL of 1 M ammonia contains 14 g nitrogen
20 mL of 1 M ammonia contains $\frac{{(14 \times 20g)}}{{1000}}$ nitrogen
% of nitrogen = $ \frac{{(14 \times 20 \times 100)}}{{1000 \times 0.5}}$ = 56.0%

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct IUPAC name of the given compound?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is the correct IUPAC name of the given compound?
questions.explanation: In IUPAC name, functional groups are written in alphabetical order. Locant 3 is assigned to ethyl group and the two methyl groups are present on C-4. The longest C chain has 7 C atoms so the IUPAC name is 3-ethyl-4,4-dimethylheptane.

questions.question: When inductive and electromeric effects operate in opposite directions, what happens?
questions.question_raw: When inductive and electromeric effects operate in opposite directions, what happens?

options.label: D
options.text: inductive and electromeric effects operate with the same magnitude.

questions.explanation: Electromeric Effect (E effect) is a temporary effect. The organic compounds having multiple bonds (a double or triple bond) show this effect in the presence of an attacking reagent only. It is defined as the complete transfer of a shared pair of $\pi$-electrons to one of the atoms joined by multiple bonds on the demand of an attacking reagent. Polarisation of $\sigma$-bond caused by the polarisation of adjacent $\sigma$-bond is referred to as the inductive effect. When inductive and electromeric effects operate in opposite directions then electromeric effect predominates.

questions.question: What does the following diagram showing Lewis structures depict?
questions.question_raw: What does the following diagram showing Lewis structures depict?
questions.explanation: These are resonating structures of nitromethane. It is known that the two N-O bonds of nitromethane are of the same length (intermediate between a N-O single bond and a N=O double bond). The actual structure of nitromethane is, therefore, a resonance hybrid of the two canonical forms I and II.

questions.question: Nucleophile is a species that should have:
questions.question_raw: Nucleophile is a species that should have:

options.label: B
options.text: a pair of electrons to donate

correct_option_texts: a pair of electrons to donate
questions.explanation: A nucleophile means a nucleus loving species. It can be a neutral molecule with a pair of electron to donate or it can be a negatively charged species.

questions.question: In Carius method of estimation of halogen, 0.15 g of an organic compound gave 0.12 g of AgBr. Find out the percentage of bromine in the compound.
questions.question_raw: In Carius method of estimation of halogen, 0.15 g of an organic compound gave 0.12 g of AgBr. Find out the percentage of bromine in the compound.
questions.explanation:
Molar mass of AgBr = 108 + 80 = 188 g/mol
188 g AgBr contains 80 g bromine
0.12 g AgBr contains (80 $\times$ 0.12)/188 g bromine
Percentage of bromine $ = \frac{{(80 \times 0.12 \times 100)}}{{(188 \times 0.15)}} = 34.04\% $

questions.question: In Duma's method for estimation of nitrogen, 0.3 g of an organic compound gave 50 mL of nitrogen collected at 300K temperature and 715 mm pressure. The percentage composition of nitrogen in the compound (Aqueous tension at 300K = 15mm) is
questions.question_raw: In Duma's method for estimation of nitrogen, 0.3 g of an organic compound gave 50 mL of nitrogen collected at 300K temperature and 715 mm pressure. The percentage composition of nitrogen in the compound (Aqueous tension at 300K = 15mm) is
questions.explanation:
Volume of nitrogen collected at 300 K and 715 mm Hg= 50 mL
Actual pressure = 715 - 15 = 700 mm Hg
Volume of nitrogen at STP = $\frac {(273 \times 700 \times 50)}{(300 \times 760)}$ = 41.9 mL
22400 ml of nitrogen at STP weighs 28g
So 41.9 ml of nitrogen at STP weighs = $\frac {(28 g \times 41.9mL)}{22400}$ mL
Percentage of N_2 = $\frac {(28 \times 41.9 \times 100)}{(22400\times0.3)}$=17.46%

questions.question: On complete combustion, 0.246 g of an organic compound gave 0.198g of carbon dioxide and 0.1014g of water. Determine the percentage composition of carbon and hydrogen in the compound.
questions.question_raw: On complete combustion, 0.246 g of an organic compound gave 0.198g of carbon dioxide and 0.1014g of water. Determine the percentage composition of carbon and hydrogen in the compound.
questions.explanation:
Molar mass of carbondioxide=44 g/mol
Molar mass of C=12 g/mol
44 g CO_​​​​​2 contains 12 g C
0.198 g of CO_​​​​​2 contains (12 x 0.198 g)/44 C
% of C= (12x0.198x100)/(44x0.246)=21.95%
Molar mass of water=18 g/mol
18 g water contains 2 g H
0.1014 g water contains (2x0.1014 g)/18H
% of H= (2 x 0.1014 x 100)/(0.246 x 18)=4.58 %

questions.question: One among the below options which is not detected by Lassaigne’s test?
questions.question_raw: One among the below options which is not detected by Lassaigne’s test?
questions.explanation: Nitrogen, sulphur, halogens and phosphorus present in an organic compound are detected by “Lassaigne’s test”. Oxygen cannot be detected by this test.

questions.question: In the resonance structures of CH_2 = CH - CHO given below, indicate relative stability of these structures.
questions.question_raw: In the resonance structures of CH2 = CH - CHO given below, indicate relative stability of these structures.
questions.explanation: I > II> III . (III) is unstable as O which is an electronegative atom carries positive charge. (I) is stable because of more number of covalent bonds which means it is lowest in energy.
